volume.p:
The following is a general sketch of the important inscriptions included in the present
volume of South Indian Inscriptions.

Maurya.-Inscription No. 44 from Rājula-maṇḍagiri contains the Minor Rock Edicts I&II of Asoka,
who was in many inscriptions referred to as devānāṁpiya. His edicts describe the Dhaṁma proposed
by him which consists of not only the Buddhist doctrine, but the universal principles and virtues. In
MRE I, he says that he was an upāsaka, a lay follower of Buddha for more than two years and a
half; and was intimately associated with the Buddhist saṅgha for more than a year and was working
hard for the cause of Dharma. It is said that even a poor man can attain heaven, if he exerts in
the practice of Dhaṁma. The edict was issued when the king was on tour for 256 days.

In MRE II, the king addresses the officials, Mahāmātras employed in the empire to see that
his pronouncements on Dhaṁma reach people through Rajjukas and Raṭhikas. He says that the Dhaṁ
ma consists of obedience to one's parents and elders and speaking the truth (See detailed notes in Ep.
Ind., XXXI, pp. 211 ff).

Śātavāhana.-Inscription No. 66, hailing from Amarāvati registers a gift (details not readable) by
Gautamīputra Yajña Sātakarṇi (27th ruler of the Andhra Śātavāhanas, according to the Puranic lists).
This would be the second inscription of Yajña Sātakarṇi, the first being the China-Ganjam inscription
of the King (edited by Dr. G. Buhler in Ep. Ind., Vol. I). The inscription refers to the Dhanakaṭa-
Chaitya. It is usually said that the Chaitya received the glorious state and splendor during Yajña-
Śātakarṇi's reign.

Ikshvāku.-Inscription No. 265 refers to the installation of a stone image of Buddha by Kumāranandin,
who was a Śrēshṭhin, at the Mahādēvi-parivēṇa at Vijayapurī on the Śrīparvata. This was dated in the
24th regnal year of Ehavala Chāntamūla (See Ep. Ind., XXXV, pp. 11 ff). The donor was described as
a member of the family of Īru[sakā]nāṁ Baraṇakīyānāṁ. The last name reminds us of the Pūgiya
family which is mentioned in one of the Nāgārjunakoṇḍa inscriptions.

Another inscription, No. 263 records the erection of a memorial pillar in the name of
Vaṁmabhaṭā of the Bahaphala-gōtra, the daughter of one Mahākshatrapa and the mother (or step-
mother) of Vāsishṭhīputra Rudrapurushadatta (See Ep. Ind., Vol. XXXIV, pp. 20 ff). The inscription
is dated in the 11th year of the King. It shows that the earlier matrimonial alliance between the
Ikshvākus and Śakas of Avanti continued during this period also. It may be recalled that one of
the queens of Vīrapurushadatta was a daughter of the ruler of Ujjain. In the same way Ehuvula
Chāntamūla must have married this lady Vaṁmabhaṭā. She bears the gōtra-name of Bahaphala. It is
not clear whether this family could be related to Jayavarman of the Bṛihatphalāyana family, which
rose to power in the Krishna valley in succession to the Ikshvākus. The title svāmin prefixed to the
names of the Ikshvāku Kings signifies the Western Kshatrapa influence on the Ikshvākus.

Post-Mauryan (mostly label) records up to A.D. 300.-There are a large number of records, which
do not refer to any king or officers who could be identified with the known persons. Nor are they
dated in a known era, so that palaeography is the only recourse for dating them. A large number of
them come from pot-sherds discovered in the excavations at Sālihuṇḍām. Others come from Amarāvati
and Nāgārjunakoṇḍa. These are almost label inscriptions giving the name of the donor and sometimes
the nature of the gift.

The Sālihuṇḍām stone-step inscription records the name of Asoka-siri and describes him as
Dhaṁma-raño in the Brāhmī script of the 2nd century A.D. (No. 81; Ep. Ind., XXXI pp. 87-88). One
refers to Bamhaṇa (or a Brāhmaṇa) who was the donor of the pot. (No. 101) That Sālihuṇḍām, a
Buddhist site received patronage of Brāhmaṇas stands revealed from this record. Another mentions
(paṁcha-vīra) pañcha-vīra (No. 108) which may be connected with the Bhāgavata cult, which
transformed itself from Pañcha-vīra to Chatur-vyūha. One (No. 103) records the names of Digha and
Majhima (nikāyas) of Buddhist Canon. Another refers to Mahāvagga. There is a reference to gahapati,
(No. 114) so common during the Sātavāhana period. One pot-sherd (No. 113) refers to Sālipēḍaka
Chēdi(ti)ya which shows the earlier name of Sālihuṇḍām. It also speaks of a gō-valava (gō-vallabha),
an officer in-charge of cattle. This name figures as gō-vallabha, along with Vallabha in the Sanskrit
Charters of the Early Pallavas.

Inscriptions at Nāgārjunakoṇḍa show that the apara-mahāvinaseliyas were located at this
place. It is well-known that there were also pubba (pūrva)-śailīyas along with the aparavina-saleyas
in Andhra. These sects must have been named after east and west to the centre at Dhanakaṭa
(Amarāvati)-Chaitya. An inscription from Amarāvati of 2nd century B.C. speaks of the attendants of
a princess Sammali as donors.

Some of the Nāgārjunakoṇḍa inscriptions record the death of warriors in a battle which
ensued between the Ikshvākus and some outside power. Many of the deceased are said to have been
residents of Maṅgalaraṇa. One is stated to be Sēnāpati Karadaru-Kumāra (prince) probably related to
the Ikshvākus (No. 268). One was Chaṁtapula whose name comes nearer to Chaṁtamūla. He was
described as a mahāsenāpati and an expert in seizing elephants (No. 264). There is another memorial
pillar recording the death of a raṭhika (rāshṭrika) who was a resident of Magalaraṇa (No. 271). Another
raṭhika refigures in the inscription No. 270.

It is quite possible that many of these commanders fell on the side of the Ikshvākus in the
battle fought most probably with the Ābhīras, aided by the Śaka ruler of Avanti and Chuṭu ruler
of Banavāsi.

Chāḷukya-Chōḷa.-There are two records of the Chāḷukya-Chōḷas. Inscription No. 242 belongs to the
time of Rājarāja (II) dated A.D. 1149 and records the gift of a lamp to a temple at Ryāli (East
Godavari district). It refers to two persons, Paṇḍayarāja and Komma-nāyaka, whose role in the history
of the times is not known. Another (No. 254) dated A.D. 1155 in the reign of Tribhuvana-Chakravartin
Kulōttuṅga Chōḷa (II) hails from Uṇḍavalli (Guntur district) and refers to Āchārya Śrivallabha of
Uṇḍavelli.

Velanāṇḍu Chief.-There are two inscriptions of Velanāṇṭi Rājēndra Chōḍa, son of Goṅka. The
inscription hailing from Dharaṇikōṭa (No. 68) mentions the genealogy of the king and speaks of
an ancestor Mallavarman to have received Velanāṇḍu-vishaya, along with the insignia of samantas
from Triyaṁbaka Pallava. It is anyway strange that the family does not owe its rise to the Eastern
Chalukyas of Veṅgī, who were the masters of Coastal Andhra before the advent of the Velanāṇḍus.
This would indicate that parts of Nellore-Prakasam districts must have been under the control of the
Pallavas whom the ancestors of Valanāṇḍus might have helped in their wars.

Inscription No. 257, coming from Petlūrupalem, speaks of the reign of Kulōttuṅga Chōḍa in
A.D. 1153. The ruler might be Rājēndra Chōḍa. It records the construction of a tank and endowment
for havis, bali and archana of god Trikūṭēśvaradēva (of Kōṭappakoṇḍa?). The donor was a servant of
one Buddana of the Harita-gōtra.

Chakranārāyaṇa Rulers.-There is only one record of these rulers (No. 123), coming from Ēlīśvaram
which refers to gifts of two rulers. One mentions Mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Anuṅgu Kāmarāju Bētarāju
(implying that Bētarāju was the son of Anuṅga Kāmarāju) who gifted the garden of Rachcha-
mallanipalli for aṅga-raṅga-bhōgas of god Yēlīśvaradēvara. It is followed by Anuṅgurāju's gift of the
village Nārapepalli (to the same god). It is not clear whether he was the father of Bētarāju or another
member of the family. We have rulers who are termed as Chakra-nārāyaṇa princes of Addaṅki to
which family, these rulers must have belonged.

Minor Chāḷukya Families.-Inscription No. 246 from Narsapur (West Godavari district) records the gift
of a perpetual lamp (akhaṇḍa-dīpa) to the temple of Markaṇḍēśvara-mahādēva of Rājamahēndrapura
for which 50 inupa-yeḍlu (buffaloes or sheep) were given by one Pōtāsani, daughter of Pōtamāṟeni.
The cattle was entrusted to Kētana-bōya, son of Pōtana-pāṅgu for the daily supply of ghee measuring
one sāniya-mānika to the temple. This is the general nature of many of the temple inscriptions of
medieval Andhra Pradesh. Lamp has to last throughout the day in the temple for which sufficient
ghee (one māna or aḍḍa) is to be supplied by the bōyas who receive the cattle from donors.
Sometimes land-gift or cash gift is made to cover this expense.

There are different types of lighting-akhaṇḍadīpa, ara-dīpa and sandhyā-dīpa etc., with
variations of the cattle to be gifted: 50 buffaloes or 25 buffaloes etc.; or 25 cows or 12 cows, as
the case may be. A number of inscriptions at Drākshārāma, Samalkot, Bapaṭla, Vijayawada and other
places record gifts of lamps in the temple.

The above donatrix, Pōtāsāni, daughter of Pōtakaṟeni (instead of Pōtamāṟeni) figures in another
inscription at Narsapur itself dated A.D. 1298 making again a gift of 50 buffaloes to the same temple
at Rājamahēndrapura (No. 248).

The King mentioned in the inscription No. 246 was Sarvalōkāśraya Vishṇuvardhana Vīrabhadrē-
śvara Chakravarti. This could be a later Vīrabhadra of the Chāḷukyas of Niḍaḍavole other than the
husband of the famous Kākatīya queen, Rudramadēvi (1261-1289) from the fact that inscription No.
248 referring to Pōtasāni is dated A.D. 1298 which is a late date for Vīrabhadra, the Kākatīya son-
in-law.

In other inscriptions, we have the name of Sarvalōkāśraya Vishṇuvardhana, the general
designation of the Chāḷukyas of Vēṅgī. It is not possible to identify them, except that they belong to
the Later Chāḷukyas of Vēṅgī, like those of Niḍadavōle, Jananāthapura and other places. Inscription
No. 253 from Uṇḍavalli (Guntur district) mentions that Bhīmarāja of Kaṇḍravāḍi installed Bhīmēśvara
in the temple; constructed by Paṇḍireḍḍi during the time of Sarvalōkāśraya Vishṇuvardhana.

Later Eastern Gaṅga.-Inscription No. 115 dated A.D. 1088 belonging to the time of Anantavarman
Chōḍa Gaṅga records the installation of a Vishṇu shrine. Another (No. 58) records the installation
of a deity and gift of a perpetual lamp to a temple by a queen of Chōḍa-Gaṅga. This temple is
located in Sāgara-Khonna-vishaya. Khonna-vishaya is already known from the Gaṅga inscriptions.
The Maḍutuṟṟu inscription of Anantavarman Rājarāja III (No. 241) has been published in Ep. Ind.,
XLI, pp. 241 ff. Dated in A.D. 1197, it records the installation of, and land-gifts to a deity called
Chōḍīśvara-mahādēva, probably after the well-known ruler Chōḍa-Gaṅga. It records the land-grants
for maintaining services in the temple. The donors were Prōli-seṭṭi and Nāmi-seṭṭi who belonged to
seṭṭikāra, probably a merchant-guild.

Inscription numbers 306, 307 and 308, coming from Chiṭṭivalasa (Visakhapatnam district) refer
to the rule of Anaṅtavarman Chōḍa Gaṅga with the usual praśasti samara-mukh-ānēka-ripu-darppa-
marddana • • • parama-māhēśvara-parama-bhaṭṭāraka etc. They record gifts to the Gōkarṇēśvaradēva
temple, located at Guḍivāḍa (Visakhapatnam district). The first one (No. 306) refers to a share of
income from a certain levy for maintenance of perpetual lamps in the temple. It also refers to a
place Barika (?) which is otherwise known as Uttama-Gaṅga-dēśiyya-Koṇḍa-paṭṭaṇa which must have
been an important trade centre. It is known that Visakhapatnam was rechristened as Kulōttuṅga
Chōḷa-paṭṭana and Moṭupalli as dēśiyyukoṇḍa-pattaṇa at a later date. No. 307 refers to the gift of
some cultivable land for food-offerings to Gōkarṇa-Rājanārāyaṇēśvara-mahādēva of Guḍivāḍa by one
Kommana with the consent of Jaddepa-nāyaka, son of Chāmi-nāyaka. Jaddepa figures as Jeddama-
nāyaka in inscription No. 308. He must have been an official or lord of a locality, as he gifts the
village under his enjoyment (bhukti) to god Gōkarṇēśvara.

Kākatīya.-A number of inscriptions from Ēlīśvaram (Nalgonda District) refer to the reigns of
Gaṇapatidēva and Rudradēva (Rudradēvī). Of them, inscription No. 116 dated A.D. 1246 of the time
of Gaṇapatidēva endows him with a big royal praśasti, which is generally absent in most of his
records. As is well-known that the Kākatīyas, unlike their masters, the Chāḷukyas of Kalyāṇa, satisfied
themselves with the simple title of mahāmaṇḍalēśvara. However, the copper-plate inscriptions and some
of the stone inscriptions describe them as mahārājas, without the title mahāmaṇḍalēśvara. Some of
the inscriptions of Gaṇapati, in particular, connect him with the solar family in which Rama was
born. Here a string of epithets, probably the praśasti recited in public appearances of the king, gives
Gaṇapati the higher title rājādhirāja and a number of titles like rāja-ratna-bhūshaṇa, rāja-turaga-
Rēvanta, rāja-rakshāmaṇi etc., while mentioning the lower title mahāmaṇḍalēśvara immediately before
his name. The inscription actually records that the King granted the royal share on lands irrigated by
a tank to a deity at Ēlīśvaram. Inscription (No. 11) also praises him as Sītāpati, Vidyāpati and Kaḷāpati.
Inscription No. 259 records the construction of a big tank in Uppuguṇḍūru by Aitama-seṭṭi, who was
the prabhu-mukhya of Nellūru-staḷa belonging to Pākanāḍu, 21000, for the merit of Gaṇapatidēva. In
the medieval period, in Andhra and Karnataka, one finds territorial designations suffixed by numerals.
What the numerals represent is a matter of conjecture. Some believed it was the revenue in terms
of coins. Elsewhere Pākanāḍu is said to have consisted of 21,000 grāmas which is unacceptable.
There are high numbers and how numbers ranging from 96,000 to 12. They may be revenue units.
Inscription No. 122 introduces two commanders of elephant forces (gaja-sāhiṇis) viz., Vāgeyadēva
and Gaṇapaya, who gifted land which was entrusted to the pontiff (or priest of the temple) viz.,
Bhāvabrahma-gurudēva Sahadēva. Another inscription (No. 128) refers to one Sahadēva-kumāra, who
may or may not be associated with Sahadēva of inscription No. 122.

Rudradēva or Rudramadēvī has two inscriptions in this collection. One (No. 119) states that
village Aṁbalipalli was gifted for aṅga-raṅga-bhōgas etc. of Yēlēśvaradēvara. No. 129 gives the name
of Prōla II, Gaṇapati and Rudradēvi, but the portion relating to the grant is not available. Of the
inscriptions of Pratāparudra's time, inscription No. 258 mentions one Rudranāyaṅka and describes
him as Śrīman-mahārāya-gajasāhiṇi which indicates that he was the Commander of elephant forces
of the King, though he does not mention the name of the ruler. The fact that his master was
called mahārāya shows the imperial authority of the Kākatīyas, though they styled themselves as
mahāmaṇḍalēśvaras showing humility (Inscription No. 116).

Inscription No. 294 refers to an officer who gifted land for a garden for the deity Akkaladēvi,
taking the orders from Pōtaya-sāhiṇi. A sarva-mānya grant is mentioned consisting of plots of land
in three villages to God Svayaṁbhūdēva of Darśi by one Nagādēvarāja. Inscription No. 53 dated A.D.
1315 records the gift by one Nāgaya, son of Haridēva, who was a gaṇa-bhṛitya, to goddess Irukalā-
paramēśvarī. We do not know whether he belonged to some military establishment. Inscription No. 50
dated A.D. 1322 makes mention of a gift to god Kalikēśvaradēva of Kandukūru. Lastly, there is an
inscription from Ēlīśvaram (No. 133) mentioning the gift of 5 ghaṭṭi-pahiṁḍi to god Ēlēśvara. This
type of coin might be equated with ghaṭṭi-varāha or Doḍḍa-varāha of the Vijayanagara gold coins.

Seuṇa.-There is a solitary inscription (No. 127) relating to the reign of Siṁhaṇa in this series. It
records the gift of a village in Sindavāḍi-1000 by one mahāpradhāni sarvādhikāri Sāṁva(ba)-paṇḍita
for worship and offerings of the god Ēlēśvara, with provision for a choultry. The gift seems to
have been made over to one Bhāva-brahma who might be the same as Bhāva-brahma gurudēvara
Sahadēvara of the inscription of Gaṇapatideva at the same place (Ēlīśvaram, Nalgonda district) (No.
122).

Telugu-Chōḍas of Kandūr.-We have two inscriptions of Gokarṇadēva Chōḍa mahārāja (Nos. 120
and 130) and one of Bhīmadēva Chōḍa mahārāja (No. 126), all from Ēlīśvaram (Nalgonda district).
Out of these No. 130 is informative about the King in the praśasti. It describes him as of the Solar
race, of Kāśyapa-gōtra, belonging to the line of Karikāla. He was mahāmaṇḍalēśvara and ruler of
Kōḍūru. The inscription is dated the Chāḷukya-Vikrama year 33, corresponding to A.D. 1109. He must
have been a feudatory of Vikramādītya VI, but the master's name is conspicuous by its absence.
The other two inscriptions are undated and issued independently, one by Gōkarṇadēva Chōḍa and the
other by Bhīmadēva Chōḍa.

Telugu-Chōḷas of Nellore.-Inscription No. 48 coming from Muruguḷḷa (Nellore district) makes
mention of Tirukāḷattidēva Chōḍa mahārāja as having ruled the world justly and left the world. One
of his subordinates (name unknown) made a gift of the village Muṟuguḍlu to god Viśvanāthadēva
in A.D. 1254-55.

Tirukāḷattidēva was described as of Kāśyapa-gōtra and ornament of the family of Karikāla
and Lord of Uṟaiyūr, also as Ṭenkanāditya, Kīrti-nārāyaṇa, maṁḍalika-diśāpaṭṭa, etc.

There is an inscription at Ēlīśvaram (Nalgonda district) (No. 117) which refers to a servant
of one Gaṇḍagōpāla and the king Gaṇapatidēva, which shows that Gaṇḍagōpala acknowledged the
authority of the Kākatīyas.

Telugu Chōḍas of Peḍakallu.-Inscription No. 19 coming from Ēlūru (West Godavari district) mentions
one Malla-bhūpāla of the Kāśyapa-gōtra as the son of Chōḍa Malla and Sōmāṁbā. The inscription
dated in A.D. 1201 records the gift of a lamp to god Bhīmēśvara by the king. This inscription uses
the chronogram viz. guṇ-ārka-dvijapati (Ś 1123 = A.D. 1201). The date falls in the early years of the
reign of Kākati Gaṇapati (A.D. 1199 = 1261).

Vijayanagara.-Vijayanagara inscriptions contain good information relating to the political and
cultural aspects of the period. Of the four families that ruled the Vijayanagara empire, the Tuḷuva
inscriptions are in a majority, followed by the Saṅgamas. The Aravīḍus contribute two, and Sāḷuvas
one only. The important inscriptions of the period are chronologically discussed below.

(i) Saṅgama family: The earliest inscription in this series is the one from Nellore (No. 52) dated
A.D. 1364 in the rule of Vīra-Sāvaṇṇa-Voḍeya. Though this was the time of the reign of Bukka I, the
ruler does not mention the master's name, but describes himself as mahāmaṇḍalēśvara, indicative of a
subordinate status. The inscription records the decision of the nānādēśīs to grant some amount out of
the levies collected at the Friday-market (Śukaravāra-santa), in favour of goddess Erakulā-paramēśvari.
Nānādēśīs are an old mercantile and maritime guild whose presence in Andhra by 12th-13th century
is attested by inscriptions.

There are two inscriptions (Nos. 142 and 143), issued by Kumāra Hariyaṇṇa Oḍeya (Voḍaya)
son of Vīrakumāra Virupaṇṇa, dated A.D. 1380 and 1381, without mentioning the ruler Harihara II,
but issued independently. This ruler belonging to the Saṅgama family, bears the lower title of
mahāmaṇḍalēśvara, but peculiarly the imperial titles used by the Vijayanagara rulers viz. arirāya-
vībhāḷa, bāsage-tappuva-rāyaragaṇḍa and pūrva-paśchima-dakshiṇa-samudrādhipati. It is quite possible
that persons of the royal family, entrusted with higher responsibilities, could have been permitted to
use these titles. In the inscription No. 143, the land-gift was exempted from all infringements (sarva-
bādhā-parihāramu). The general term that was used in Vijayanagara inscriptions is sarva-mānya (total
exemption). The term used here reminds us of the terms in early Andhra copper-plate grants like
sarva-kara-parihara and sarva-pīḍā-vivar jitaṁ and others. It denotes that the taxes to be paid, and the
services to be rendered, by the people of ordinary villages to the State, are waived in this case.

There is an inscription of Harihara II dated A.D. 1385 (No. 43) where he was given the title
mahāmaṇḍalēśvara, while in many inscriptions, he was endowed with imperial titles.

There are two inscriptions of Dēvarāya I (Nos. 281 and 298). The former from
Giddalūru (Kurnool district) peculiarly adds uttara to the title pūrva-paśchima-dakshiṇa and describes
him as Chatus-samudrādhīśvara. This is reminiscent of the ancient rulers like Samudragupta describing
themselves as Chatur-udadhi-salil-āsvādita-yaśasa, while the ocean on three sides of the land reminds
us of the title attributed to Gautamīputra-Śātakarṇi (ti-samuda-toya-pītavāhana) in the Nāsik inscription
of his son Vāsishṭhīputra Puḷumāvi dated in his 19th regnal year (more famously known as the Nāsik
praśasti). Inscription No. 298 speaks of one Kampadēvani Chāma Voḍeya as the ruler of Donakoṇḍa,
while Dēvarāya was ruling. It is also said that all the fifty-six countries were prospering during his
reign. The fifty-six countries are also known by the term Chhappanna-dēśalu (Inscription No. 302).

Inscription No. 300 introduces Dēvarāya II with the imperial titles as well as mahāmaṇḍalēśvara.
Again one Rāmayadēva, the donor in the inscription is also described as mahāmaṇḍalēśvara and as
the recipient of nāyaṅkara of Pina-Ēṟuva from the king.

Inscription No. 13 dated A.D. 1467 which falls in the reign of Virūpāksha II, makes a
reference to the village Mēḍēkūrti lying in the Niṭṭūri-staḷa, which was received as nāyaṅkara by
mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Mūpana-nāyaka.

An inscription (No. 1) from Achyutāpuram (Anantapur district), should also be assigned to
the Saṅgama times. It mentions a gift of land to SankīrtanaTimmaya by one Kaśivi-reḍḍi for
the merit of his superior Mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Saṁbeṭa Tirumalayyadēva-mahārāja. The reference
to SankīrtanaTimmaya might indicate that he was a musician like the famous Sankīrtanāchārya
Annamayya. He could be a contemporary of Annamayya. About the Sambeṭas and many other small
families that ruled as chiefs under Vijayanagara, more work needs to be done. The present collection
brings out a few families that have not received the attention of scholars.

(ii) Sāḷuva family: The solitary inscription of this period relates to the reign of Immaḍi Narasiṁ
ha dated A.D. 1496 (No. 2). It registers the gift of a village lying in the Niṭṭūri-sīma under Chirabaḍi.
The latter does not have any suffix indicative of a higher territorial division. Again the present
Niṭṭūri-sīma is mentioned as Niṭṭūri-staḷa in inscription No. 13.

Like many inscriptions of this period, the present inscription uses the term ashṭa-bhōga-tēja-
svāmyaṁ and specifies what those eight are. In other inscriptions in this collection, either the general
term or a few of the items are mentioned (Ins. Nos. 5, 16, 29, 31, 47 and 261). The enjoyment of
what is available on the land and what could be produced, water-sources, stones (or minerals) which
may be discovered in future come under this category which connotes full ownership of land/village.

(iii) Tuḷuva family: There are four inscriptions belonging to the time of Kṛishṇadēvarāya.
Inscription No. 261 states that one Basavā-nāyani, son of Śaṅkha-nāyani obtained Nāgārjunikoṇḍa-
sīma from SāḷuvaTimmarasa (the prime-minister of Kṛishṇarāya). Inscription No. 279 dated A.D. 1525
mentions one Kāki Appayyadēva-mahārāja, described as mahāmaṇḍalēśvara, without mentioning the
name of Kṛishṇarāya. He bears the title Gaja-daḷa-vibhāḷa (destroyer of elephant forces) which might
indicate the part played by him in defeating the Gajapati ruler Pratāparudra. As the inscription hails
from Giddalūru in Kurnool district, he must have received the nāyaṅkara in the nearby areas.

In an inscription dated A.D. 1525 coming from Donakoṇḍa (in the Nellore district) (Inscription
No. 302), King Kṛishṇarāya is described, along with the ancestral titles of mūru-rāyara-gaṁḍa etc., as
Yavana-rājya-sthāpanāchārya and the unusual epithet aśvapati-gajapati-vībhāḷa. He placed the Bahmani
ruler on his throne and took the title that he established the Muslim Kingdom. But the other title,
including Muslim rulers and the Gajapatis in one epithet is not met with in the inscriptions. As early
as the time of the Hampi inscription of January 1510, the king bears the separate titles indicative of
his victory over the Gajapatis and Muslims (Gajapati-gajakūṭa-pākala and paribhūta-suratrāṇa). This
inscription interestingly speaks of some officers of the king, who appealed to the king for grant of
the village Donakoṇḍa to the deity Gaṅgā-paramēśvari. They seem to be of Yādava-kula; and they are
described as yadukula-śrēshṭhas and gōpakulavardhanas. Some of them like Timmapa-nāyaka, Aḍapa-
nāyaka and Narasapa-nāyaka were dvārapālas (door-keepers); one was Bhaṇḍāraṁ Peddapa-nāyaka;
another was Bokkasam Bairinēḍu and still another Nāgatāta; and avasaram Basivinēḍu and Kētayya.
These are very important officials of the period; and it has to be seen whether they figure in the
already published inscriptions. Another important feature of the inscription is that it adds vāpī-kūpa-
taṭāka to the list of eight enjoyments (ashṭa-bhōgas) cited in other Vijayanagara records. The grant of
the village is said to have been declared in the presence of the deities Virūpāksha and Viṭhalēśvara
at Hampi, to indicate the sanctity of the gift.

Inscription No. 54, coming from Niḍigallu (Nellore district) brings to light some more officers
of Kṛishṇarāya's time. Ingumānēni was the Kāryakarta of one Tirumala-nāyini, son of Yeggappa-nāyini
who was the officer-in-charge of the fort of Chandragiri. Kṛishṇarāya's name appears in this inscription
with the lower epithet mahāmaṇḍalēśvara, while the royal titles like rājādhirāja and rājaparamēśvara
were also used. This is a rare use of the lower title for the king.

Among the inscriptions of Sadāśivarāya, three inscriptions coming from Niṭṭūr (Anantapur
district) should be studied together. The donor of the three records was Jakkaṁpūḍi Tirumala-nāyani
(Inscription Nos. 14, 15 and 16), son of Peddi-nāyani. He belonged to the Valluṭla-gōtra according to
the inscription Nos. 14 and 16, while it was Achyuta-gōtra according to inscription No. 15. It is not
clear why this discrepancy occurred. As per the inscription No. 15, he is supposed to have received the
nāyaṅkara of Niṭṭūr in Chirabaḍi-sīma from the king. No. 16 however makes it clear that he received
Niṭṭūr as an uṁbaḷi from Pemmasāni Rāmaliṅganāyani, the latter himself having obtained nāyaṅkara
from Mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Rāmarāju-Tirumalarājayyadēva. Inscription No. 15 dated A.D. 1544 registers a
gift made for the merit of Sadāśivarāya, (Aḷiya) Rāmarāja and Pemmasāni China Timmānāyani. This
shows that he owed his position to China Timmā-nayani or Rāmaliṅga of the Pemmasāni family. It
is possible that Chirabaḍi-sīma was obtained from Rāmarāja Tirumalarājayya by the Pemmasānis under
whom Jakkaṁpūḍi Tirumala-nāyani was functioning. This very clearly indicates sub-infeudation with
the following hierarchy:
i) Sadāśivarāya as king;
ii) Rāmarāja-Tirumalarājayya as governor or chief, with control over feudatories;
iii) Pemmasāni Rāmaliṅga receives nāyankara from (ii);
iv) Jakkaṁpūḍi. Tirumalanāyani receives nāyaṁkara or uṁbaḷi from (iii).

Inscription Nos. 14 and 16 give elaborately the festivals to be conducted by the temple for
which lands were assigned. These remind us of the inscriptions of Tirumala-Tirupati which give details
of the festivals and the items to be offered on such occasions. A number of festivals like teppa-
tiruṇālu, tēru-tiruṇālu, pāruvēṭa and those in Kārtika and Dhanur-māsas, pallakī-sēva, Bhāgavata-sēva,
uṭla-tiruṇālu, daśamis and pūrṇimas etc., are mentioned. They include rituals like mantra-pushpam and
food-offerings like amṛitapaḍi.

Inscription No. 14 contains one peculiar feature which is not found in Andhra inscriptions.
It refers to paṁti or paṁtina in between groups of lines. Actually, it makes paṁtina 2 after line 11
and paṁti 4 after 41. In the present day, paṁkti means line, while it also means 10 lines, according
to Sanskrit-English Dictionary by V.S. Apte. Inscriptions refer to paṁkti denoting numeral 10 in the
method of chronograms used in Andhra inscriptions. Though the separation of lines is not correct in
this inscription, the conceptual denomination of 10 lines into a category should have been considered.

There is a set of three inscriptions (Nos. 30, 32 and 40) which mention one mahānāyaṅkāchārya,
Malli-nāyini. The first two inscriptions prefix Kaṁb(h)ālapāṭi to his name, while No. 40 calls him
Rājavōli Mallinēni. From the reference to the name of the office he held, he should be identified
with the one in Nos. 30 and 32.

The first inscription dated A.D. 1565 (No. 32) registers Malli-nayani's gift of mānya land,
given to him by the Reḍḍis and people of Lakuma-sāgaram, for offerings and services to Eṟṟaguḍi
Rāmēśvaradēva. The second inscription dated A.D. 1567 (No. 30) states that Mallināyini settled the
dispute between the residents of Gummakoṇḍa and Teliki-maṇdavāḍa, who gave a land as remuneration
to him, which was gifted to the same god Rāmēśvaradēva. This makes it clear that he was a judge
who settled the land or boundary disputes. The gift of land by the villagers of Lakuma-sāgaram also
should belong to this category. In this third inscription (No. 40) Rājavolu Malli-nēni fixed some rates
of the lease of lands at Vulucharla.

Now, the term mahānāyaṅkāchārya might be taken to be a judge or tribunal, appointed for
a particular purpose. He must have been receiving such grants of land as remuneration, as a result
of which he might have made a donation of some lands to a deity.

Inscription No. 148 states that Baicharājayya was administering over Udayagiri-rājya, which
was the nāyaṅkara of mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Rāmarāju yaṟam Tirumalayyadēva. This shows that the
brother of Aḷiya Rāmarāya was holding an important rājya under whom Baicharājayya functioned.

A peculiar practice we find in the inscriptions of Sadāśivarāya is that Aḷiya Rāmarājayya's
brothers Tirumala and Veṅkaṭādri and other members of the Āravīḍu family who held offices during
this time, prefix the name of Rāmarāja to their names. Otherwise the first name should refer to the
father of the concerned individual.

Inscription No. 29 refers to a mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Pōnamarāja, son of Pōchirāja-Rāmarāju
belonging to the solar race and Viśvāmitra-gōtra, gifting a village in Dōni-sīma. Inscription No. 292
states that one mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Timmayadēva mahārāja of Ātreya-gōtra obtained Peḍakaṇṭi-sīma as
nāyaṅkara from the king. Inscription No. 31 refers to the gift-village Eṟṟaguḍi as lying in Kōṭi-kōṇa-
sīma, headed by mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Koṇḍrājayyadēva mahārāja, son of Timmarāja and grandson of
Koṇḍreḍḍi of the Gobbūri family. It may be noted that Gobbūri Jaggarāya played an important role
in the civil war period (1614-19) after the death of Veṅkaṭapatirāya (A.D. 1586-1614).

Inscription No. 41 mentions the Vipravinōdins and the grant of Vipravinōdi-vartana received
from the agrahāra to the local deity. There is a doubt whether these people were entertainers of
Brahmins or Brahmin entertainers; they were both because they received the amounts for their
services from the agrahārikas and also they refer to their gōtra and Śākhā as Brāhmaṇas do.

There is a bhaṭa-mānya grant made to one Tippa-bhaṭṭaya by a mahāmaṇḍalēśvara (Inscription
No. 43). It is not clear as to how the bhaṭa-mānya differs from an agrahāra. Could it be a
personal grant? The agrahāra was invariably given collectively to Brahmins, who were specialists
in various fields of learning. The inscription also speaks of the donor mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Māḍrāju
Narasarājayyadēva-mahārāja as having received the village Vuluchala lying in Kandanōli-Sīma as
amara-nāyaṅkara from Rāmarāju-Veṅkaṭādri rājayyadēva-mahārāja (Veṅkaṭādri, the youngest brother
of Aḷiya Rāmarāya).

(iv) Āravīḍu family.-Inscription No. 12 is dated in the reign of Veṅkaṭapatirāya (A.D. 1611). A
certain Paruvata-nāyani, son of Tiruveṅgaḷappa gave over the money derived from the mānya lands
of the temple to god Kāśi-viśvēśvara for the merit of Pemmasāni Timma-nāyani, which shows his
subordination to the Pemmasāni chief.

Inscription No. 149 coming from Saṅgam (Nellore district) makes mention of the consecration
of the liṅga of Kāśi-viśvanātha in the reign of Komāra Veṅkaṭrāyalu. He should be the same as
Veṅkaṭa III (1630-1642). But the date of the inscription being 1617 and the assumption of the term
Komāra or Kumāra, he should be regarded as heir-apparent during the reign of Rāmadēvarāya (1619-
1630). It is interesting that though he was the heir-apparent, he holds the royal titles of rājādhirāja
and rājaparamēśvara.

Miscellaneous.-An undated inscription, but palaeographically attributable to 16th-17th century (Inscription
No. 11) states that that stone marks the boundary for three villages viz., Kallūru, Rāmabhaṭlayyavāri-
agrahāram and Ālākoṇḍa. It is also mentioned that Kallūru is also known as Śrīraṅgarājapuram, and
Ālākoṇḍa is known as Koṇḍasamudram-Vēlūr.

Inscription No. 18 says that one Peda-Basavaya erected a water-shed (chali-vendara). Nos. 36
and 37 speak of one person Nadala or Nedham Yallappa who gave yedureḍa to certain persons. No.
36 states that Yallappa made a declaration saying that he received back the money towards the loan
given to Mālakoṇḍa Timmarāja. No. 37 states that the same Yallappa declared that he received the
principal and the interest thereon on the loan he gave to one Basuvaya and some Kāpus. As the
loan papers that should lie with the creditor are probably lost or not traceable, he issued this public
declaration that the concerned persons do not owe him anything and the loan amount is discharged.
Even if the papers are found at a later date, he declares that he has no claim, since the amounts
are paid. This public declaration by means of inscriptions makes the people of the village witnesses
to the declaration of the creditor. The ‘yedureda’ that he has given seems to be a discharge certificate
for the loan-taker.

Inscription No. 39 refers to a grant of 20 maruturu of land to god Takshakēśvara-bhaṭāra
of Nariyanūru (Kurnool district) by munnūrruvar. The inscription bears no date; and palaeographical
features point to 10th century. Inscriptions of Andhra Pradesh attest to the presence of munnūrruvuru
from 12th century A.D. They may relate to ayyas or sanis in the temple. This seems to be the earliest
reference to the organization, as palaeographically, the inscription is to be dated to the 10th century
A.D. The inscription also mentions the term Vēṭu-lēkuṁḍa (without any levies or conditions?) (line
4), which might indicate that the land was fully exempted from all dues to the State (sarva-kara-
parihāra, sarva-mānya and the like). Inscription No. 79 refers to an agreement reached between two
parties, which is called vorima-patraṁ. In a similar way, Inscription no. 140 states that there was an
agreement (voppi) between the chief and the local Kāpus and Karaṇas. Inscription No. 141 speaks
of gifts of land, along with the incomes due to the state (sakal-āya-sahitamu).

No. 288 mentions utpatti-piḍugu, which occurs in records of 7th-8th century A.D. The
inscription is palaeographically assigned to 7th century A.D. It may refer to a title. Inscription No. 47
dated in A.D. 1697 registers a gift to the temple of Chenna-kēśava at Chēṁjerla and to Nāgavōjhula
-Timmāvōjhula by Vēmā-reḍḍi of Sarvapalli, who holds Cheṁjerla in Udayagiri-rājya as nāyaṅkara.
The presence of this small ruler shows that the earlier rulers/chieftains might have been permitted to
rule under the Mughal rule. It may be noted that the Qutb Shāhi rule ended in 1687, and Andhra
came under the Mughals for sometime. Interestingly, Vēmā Reḍḍi holds the title “Hindurāya-suratrāṇa”.

Inscription No. 35 makes mention of the consecration of a Śiva temple, erection of a pillar of
victory and celebration of vasantōtsava to god Ahōbalēśvara by one Narasimha-pātruḍu. The setting
up of a victory-pillar and the assumption of the title pātruḍu by this individual tempts one to regard
him as a military general of the Gajapati ruler Kapilēśvara (A.D. 1435-68) whose campaigns against
the Saṅgama rulers are well-recorded. The occurrence of the word fouj in the inscription is strange,
because inscriptions of 15th-16th century do not generally refer to this term.

Inscription No. 294 from Darśi of the time Kākati Pratāparudra states that one Mallaṇadēva took
orders from one Pōtaya Sāhiṇi and arranged for a flower garden at the temple of Tripurāntakēśvara
at Tripurāntakaṁ and gifted land for its maintenance. In this inscription, it is stated that it was a
sarvamānya gift and that there was no paṁggamu (some levy). Dr. D.C. Sircar wrote a note on this
term in Epigraphia Indica., Vol. XXXIII, pp. 54 ff, where he has discussed the meaning of the term,
while citing a number of inscriptions from medieval Andhra and Karnataka.

inscription.head:
No. 1.
(A.R. No. B. 1 of 1953-54.)
Achyutāpuram, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone in a field.

The record is dated Pārthiva, chaitra, śu. 11, va (Saturday) and in characters of 15th century
A.D.

Registers a grant of some land to [Saṅkīrttana] Timmaya, son of Abarāju, by Chinnapa-
reḍḍi-Akkali-reḍḍi-Kas[i*]vi-reḍḍi of Chilumakūru for the merit of Mahānaṇḍaḷēśvara Saṁbeṭa
Tirumalayyadēva-mahārāja who is endowed with the titles Maṁnya-Gajapati and Aṁtyaṁbaragaṇḍa.
The donee seems to have been associated with the Yōgānanda Nārasiṁha temple and other Vaishṇava
shrines which seem to have received different plots of land at Mōṭrugaḍa. It refers to some Kānikas,
the payment of which might have been exempted to the villagers. The inscription was written by
Sūgūri Tiṁmana.

edition.p: Figures of Conch(?), tirunāmaṁ and discus(?).

l.te: kha 0 || nu yēṁṭā māku cellē rā-

edition.p: Figures of Sun and Crescent moon; below are engraved a Kalaśa(?), flanked by temples(?)
To the right of the second face, there is an inscription with the depiction of a tree.

inscription.head:
No. 2.
(A.R. No. B. 2 of 1953-54.)
Chilamakūru, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone in front of the Ellamma temple.

The record is dated Śaka 1[418] • • • • ., Māgha, ba. 30, Monday, Solar eclipse, corresponding
to A.D. 1496, February 14.

Damaged. Records the gift of the village [Kōmaṭi]guṇṭla alias Narasiṁhapuram belonging
to Niṭṭūri-sīma under Chirabaḍi-(sīma) for the merit of Kaṭhāri Sāḷuva Narasingarāya mahārāya
to goddess Akaḷā Paramēśvarī of Chilumakūru. The donor seems to be [Naṁḍāni-nāyanigāru]. The
goddess is described as ādhāra-śakti, lōka-mātā, arivīra-Aṅkāḷa-Chaṇḍī. The inscription refers to the
following villages as boundaries: Niṭṭūru, Mēḍikuṟṟu, Mallēpalli, Chāluvēmula and Puṭlūru.

edition.p: Figures of Cresent-moon, tirunāmaṁ and sun.

l.te: caṁḍḍi cilumakūri yakalā paramēśvariki yicci[na] bhūdāna
ri mēḍikuriti polaṁmēra [|*] dakṣiṇā[na*]ku mallēpalli polaṁ-
śrī śrī śrī [|*] svadattā dviguṇaṁ puṇyaṁ paradattānu-

inscription.head:
No. 3.
(A.R. No. B. 3 of 1953-54.)
Chilamakūru, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone is a field to the west of the village.

The record is dated Parīdhāvi, Kārttika, śu. 5, Thursday. Written in characters of 16th century
A.D.
Fragmentary and damaged. Details completely lost.

edition.p: Discus (?), moon & star and Conch (?)

inscription.head:
No. 4.
(A.R. No. B. 4 of 1953-54.)
Chilamakūru, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone built into a water-sluice called Raṅgappabhāvi.

The record is dated Śaka [163]6, Kshaya, Jyēshṭha, śu. [15]. The Śaka and cyclic years do not
correspond.

Fragmentary. Seems to refer to the construction of a well and to the endowment in the shape
of land made for its upkeep, by purchase from Annapā and Tippā-bhaṭla in an agrahāra (name lost).

edition.p: The figure of Sun.

inscription.head:
No. 5.
(A.R. No. B. 5 of 1953-54.)
Chintalapalle, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone in a hay-stack.
The record is dated Bhāva, Pushya, śu. 11.

Fragmentary. Seems to refer to some grant of land by a ruler (name lost) who was described
an a Mahāmaṇḍalēśvara and a member of the Āravīḍu family.

edition.p:
The rest of the record is completely effaced. There is a symbol of sun to the right of
lines 3 and 4.

inscription.head:
No. 6.
(A.R. No. B. 7 of 1953-54.)
Ellanūru, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone in the Veṅkaṭaramaṇasvāmin temple.

The record is dated Śaka 1592, Sādhāraṇa, Jyēshṭha, śu. 15, corresponding to A.D. 1670, May
24.

Incomplete. Mentions god Veṅkaṭēśvara of Velalūru and seems to refer to the ratha-
[mahōtsava*] of the deity.

edition.p: Figures of Sun, Vaḍagalai-nāmam, crescent moon.
Incomplete.

inscription.head:
No. 7.
(A.R. No. B. 8 of 1953-54.)
Ellanūru, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone in the Veṅkaṭaramaṇasvāmin temple.

The record is dated Śaka 1592, Sādhāraṇa, Jyēshṭha, śu. 15, corresponding to A.D. 1670, May
24.

Records a decision arrived at by the Reḍḍis and Karaṇas of Vellalūru and the group of
villages included in it (samuta) to subscribe at the rate of two rūkas for every hundred of the total
land assessment (sistu-bērīju) for conducting the car-festival (ratha-mahōtsava) of the deity.

edition.p: Figures of Sun, Vaḍagala-nāmam, Moon.
*) The letter is that of the Nāgarī script.

inscription.head:
No. 8.
(A.R. No. B. 10 of 1953-54.)
Ellanūru, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On the east wall of the Āñjanēya shrine in the Veṅkaṭaramaṇasvāmin temple.
The record is dated [Bhā]va, Kārttika, śu. 12 (Written in late characters).

Damaged and indistinct. Seems to record some gift of lands to god Chinttalarāya of Tāṁḍiparati
by Kṛishṇamarājulaṁgāru.

inscription.head:
No. 9.
(A.R. No. B. 10A of 1953-54.)
Ellanūru, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab covering a mori near the Idga.
The record is dated in Śaka • • • • • [Śrāvaṇa], va (Saturday) (Datable to 16th-17th century A.D.)
Much effaced. No information, either of donor or the details of the grant.

inscription.head:
No. 10.
(A.R. No. B. 11 of 1953-54.)
Ellanūru, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone in front of the Buchchamma temple in Pātapēṭa, a suburb of Ellanūru.

The record is dated Śaka 1671, Krōdhi (wrong), Jyēshṭha, śu. 15 and 1668, Śubhakṛit (wrong),
Kārttika, śu. 15. Irregular date. The second part dated Śaka 1668 should come first, followed by the
one dated Śaka 1671.

Registers a gift of one tūmu of land to Kōṭa Buchaṁma (deity or individual) of Pātapēṭa
by the Reḍḍis and Karaṇas of Kasanūru for worship and offerings of god Rāmaliṅgēśvara, newly
installed; also registers another gift of a tūmu of land to the same deity on the latter date by the
Reḍḍis and Karaṇas of Siṁhādripuram for a similar purpose.

edition.p: As a continuation of line 30, there is another inscription which is transcribed below.

inscription.head:
No. 11.
(A.R. No. B. 13 of 1953-54.)
Gaḍḍaṁvāripalle (hamlet of Mallāguṇḍla), Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District,
Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a boundary stone, east of the village.
Undated. Written in 16th-17th century A.D. characters.

States that the stone marks the boundary for three villages, viz., Kallūru alias Śrīraṅgarājapuram,
Āravīṭi Rāmā[bhaṭla]yyavāri-agrahāram and Ālākoṇḍa alias Koṇḍasamudram-Vēlūru. It is stated that
the Rāma[bhaṭla]yya-agrahāram was established by Sadāśivarāya. But it is not clear whether the stone
was erected during the period of Sadāśiva's rule (1542-1570 A.D.), or a little later.

edition.h4: TEXT.Figure ·of Sun1. kallūri grā- Figure ·of crescent-moon

l.note:
Figure of a standing saint with an
umbrella in his left hand and a Kamaṇḍu in his right hand.

inscription.head:
No. 12.
(A.R. No. B. 17 of 1953-54.)
Mallāguṇḍla, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab near the Āñjanēya temple.

The record is dated in Śaka 1534, Vīrōdhikṛit, Pushya, śu. 5. Vīrōdhikṛit corresponds to Śaka
1533. It so, the date would be A.D. 1611, December 29.

Registers a gift of money derived from the mānya lands of the temple to god Kāśī-Viśvēśvara
of Mallāguṇḍla by Paruvata-nāyuḍu, son of Tiruveṅgaḷappa and grandson of (name not clear) of the
Toṇḍipāḷḷa-gōtra for worship and offerings and for the procession of the deity along the streets. The
gift is said to have been made for the merit of Pemmasāni Timmā-nāyuḍu.

edition.p: Figures of Sun, trident, liṅga, ḍamaru and crescent-moon.

inscription.head:
No. 13.
(A.R. No. B. 20 of 1953-54.)
Mēḍikurti, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone near the Abbā-maṭha.

The record is dated Śaka 1388, Vyaya, Phālguṇa, ba. 30, solar eclipse, corresponding to A.D.
1467, March 6.

Damaged and worn out. Seems to record certain grants for worship and offerings of the local
deity. Mēḍēkurti is referred to as a Nāyaṅkara village in Niṭṭūri-sthaḷa. Refers to Mahāmaṇḍalēśvara
[Mūpana]-nāyaka and Mallayadeva mahārāja and at the end of the inscription Narasiṅga choḍa, whose
inter-relationship is not clear.

inscription.head:
No. 14.
(A.R. No. B. 21 of 1953-54.)
Niṭṭūru, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a pillar in front of the Nīlakaṇṭhēśvara temple.

The record is dated Śaka 1472 (current), Sumya, Nija Vaiśākha, śu. 1[2], Wednesday,
corresponding to A.D. 1549, May 8.

Records gift of several plots of wet and dry land in Niṭūru situated in Chirabaḍi-sīma to god
Chennakēśava for worship and offerings by Tirumala-nāyaniṁgāru, son of Pedi-nāyyani of Jakkaṁ-
pūṇḍi, who belonged to the Valluṭla-gōtra and obtained the sīma as nāyaṅkara from the king. (Also
records the gift of a plot of land by the same chief to Āribhāgam Tirumalayaṁgāru for the offering
of curd-rice to the god). It also refers to God Hanumanta.

inscription.head:
No. 15.
(A.R. No. B. 22 of 1953-54.)
Niṭṭūru, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a pillar in front of the Nīlakaṇṭhēśvara temple.

The record is dated Śaka 1466, Śobhakṛit, (Krodhin), Bhadrapada, śu. 11, corresponding to A.D.
1544, August 28.

Records the gift of some additional plots of land in Mēḍikurti to the Nīḷakaṇṭhēśvara temple
at Niṭṭūr for the merit of King Sadāśiva, Rāmarāja, Pemmasāni China Timmā nāyaka and Peddi-
nāyaka (the father of Tirumalayya) by Tirumalayya of Jakaṁpūṇḍi, belonging to the Achyuta-gōtra.
(The donor may be identical with the person mentioned in No. 14 (A.R. 21 of 1953-54) except for the
name of the gōtra). It is also stated that the temple of Nīḷakaṇṭhēśvara already obtained some land
for aṅga-raṅga-bhōga and amṛitapaḍi of the deity. It seems to have waived some tax due from the
Śrōtriyam and sthānikas of the temple.

It is significant to note that the above declaration was made by Tirumalayya on the banks
of the Tuṅgabhadrā and in the presence of god Viṭṭhalēśvara.

maṁggaḷaṁmahā śrī śrī śrī śrī śrīṁ jēyunu

inscription.head:
No. 16.
(A.R. No. B. 23 of 1953-54.)
Niṭṭūru, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab set up in front of the Chennakēśava temple.

The record is dated Śaka 1472 (current), Saumya, Śrāvaṇa, ba. 8, corresponding to A.D. 1549,
August 16.

Registers the gift of lands to god Chennakēśava of Niṭṭūru by Tirumala-nāyiniṁgāru, son
of Jakkaṁpūṇḍi Peddi-nāyiniṁgāru of Valluṭla-gōtra. He obtained village Niṭṭūru as uṁbaḷi from
Rāmaliṅga-nāyiniṁgāru, son of Pemmasāni China Timmā-nāyiniṁgāru, who obtained Chirabadi-[sīma]
as nāyaṅkara from Rāmarāja-Tirumalarājayyadēva-mahārāja. The gift was made on the occasion of
Kṛishṇa-jayanti.

The record contains two more grants made on the occasion of Kṛishṇa-jayanti, one in the
year Kāḷayukti (Śaka 1482) by Rāmaliṅga-nāyuḍu and another in the year Rākshasa (Śaka 1477) by
the Kāpus (farmers), and the Karaṇas (accountants) of Niṭṭūru, Gollapalle and Vēmulapalle, the
former granting some lands and the latter levying grain at the rate of 1 mānika for every tūmu of
the produce in the village.

The reverse of the slab gives an elaborate schedule of assignments of the gift land
for the various items of offerings during worship and for conducting Bhāgavata-sēva and the
chanting of the mantra-pushpam, Puḷukāpu on Friday, tēppa-tirunāḷotsavam (floating-festival), Pāruvēṇṭa
mahōtsavam (hunting festival) and Mahānavami festival are also mentioned. It mentions various
measures of land as well as liquid measures for measuring oil, ghee etc. Perfumes like camphor,
Kastūri and Kumkaṁ etc., used for decoration also find mention. Reference is made to dasavanda
lands.

inscription.head:
No. 17.
(A.R. No. B. 24 of 1953-54.)
Pātapalle, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone in a field to the south of the village.
The record is dated Śaka 1434, Āngi[rasa] • • • • • 15. The date could be after A.D. 1512, March 18.

Fragmentary. Seems to register a land grant in Kasavānūru by Yallama-nāyaka of Iḍumula-
gōtra to Parvatayya, Rāmayya, Jannayya and Gannayya of Ṛik-śākhā and Vishṇuvardhana-gōtra. Other
details of the record are lost.

l.te: ḍu tāllu cēnunnu dāravōśi ·istimi ·iḍumula gōtrulaina ya-

inscription.head:
No. 18.
(A.R. No. B. 25 of 1953-54.)
Pātapalle, Tadipatri Taluk, Anantapur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone in front of the Chennakēśava temple.
The record is dated Kāḷayukti, Chaitra, śu. 1. [Written in late characters].
States that the water-shed was erected by Peda-Basavaya, son of Yaṁchaya(?).

inscription.head:
No. 19.
(A.R. No. B. 26 of 1953-54.)
Ēlūru, Eluru Taluk, West Godavari District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On pillar No. 1 in the compound of the Taluk Office.

The record is dated in Śaka 1123 (Chronogram: guṇ-ārka-dvijapati), Nakra (Makara)-
saṅkrānti, corresponding to A.D. 1201, December 28.

Written in Sanskrit verse, the inscription records a gift of a lamp to god Bhīmēśvara by
Mallabhūpāla, son of Chōḍa Malla and Sōmāṁbā and grandson of (name lost) and lord of Peḍakallu.
The king is said to belong to the Kāśyapa-gōtra which is known from the Telugu-Chōḷa records.

inscription.head:
No. 20.
(A.R. No. B. 27 of 1953-54.)
Ēlūru, Eluru Taluk, West Godavari District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On pillar No. 2 in the compound of the Taluk Office.
Undated; written in characters of 12th century A.D.

Damaged. Mentions Manu (?), Rāghavēndra and Karikala, who may be considered as ancestors
of a Telugu-Choḷa family. The name of the donor is lost.

inscription.head:
No. 21.
(A.R. No. B. 28 of 1953-54.)
Ēlūru, Eluru Taluk, West Godavari District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On pillar No. 3 in the compound of the Taluk Office.
The record is dated Śaka 1181, uttarāyaṇa-saṅkrānti corresponding to A.D. 1259, December 26.

Registers a gift of ten tūmus of wet land situated in Koṭṭeguṇṭa, south of Komarapalle,
purchased from Annamarāju, for maintaining a lamp in the temple of Bhīmēśvara, east of Ḍendulū
ru by Kūchi, son of Palladāre Peggaḍa and Bonnāsāni. Records also the gift of a bronze Kaṁbham
(could it be lamp-stand?).

inscription.head:
No. 22.
(A.R. No. B. 29 of 1953-54.)
Ēlūru, Eluru Taluk, West Godavari District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On pillar No. 4 in the compound of the Taluk Office.
The record is dated Śaka 11[7]0, [Dhanus]Saṅkrānti, corresponding to A.D. 1248, November 26.
Damaged. Seems to record a gift of land to the temple of Bhīmēśvara-mahādēva.

l.te:
śakava¨rṣaṁbulu 11[7]0 lu nēṁṭṭi [dhanussu] saṁkrāṁttiya śrīmatu ḍeṁ[dulū]ri
tū¨rppuna mū-
lasthāna bhīmēśvara śrī mahādēvaraku pū[bī]kuvāru pūvalātōṁṭi • • • dīpā-

inscription.head:
No. 23.
(A.R. No. B. 30 of 1953-54.)
Ēlūru, Eluru Taluk, West Godavari District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On pillar No. 5 in the compound of the Taluk office.
The record is dated Śaka 11 • ., Makara-saṅkrānti.

Damaged. Seems to record a gift of sheep for the supply of a prescribed quantity of ghee
for a perpetual lamp in the temple of Bhīmēśvaradēva lying to the east of Dendulūru, by Māviṇṭi
Mallapa.

inscription.head:
No. 24.
(A.R. No. B. 31 of 1953-54.)
Ēlūru, Eluru Taluk, West Godavari District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On pillar No. 6 in the compound of the Taluk Office.
The record is dated Śaka • • • ., [Krōdhi]. Written in the characters of 13th-14th century A.D.
Damaged. The donor seems to be a son of • • • • • reḍi.

edition.p: [Left side of the inscription is badly damaged.]

inscription.head:
No. 25.
(A.R. No. B. 32 of 1953-54.)
Ēlūru, Eluru Taluk, West Godavari District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On pillar No. 7 in the compound of the Taluk Office.

The record is dated Śaka • • • ., Śrāvaṇa, śu. 15, lunar eclipse. Written in 12th century
characters.

Mentions [Kēdāru] Peggaḍa, sandhivigrahin (minister for war and peace) working under
Mahāmaṇḍalēśvara (name lost) who seems to have gifted sheep for a lamp in the Bhīmēśvara temple.

edition.p: Separate inscription in Sanskrit.
(Inscription damaged.)

inscription.head:
No. 26.
(A.R. No. B. 33 of 1953-54.)
Ēlūru, Eluru Taluk, West Godavari District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On pillar No. 8 in the compound of the Taluk Office.
The record is in verse and undated. Written in characters of 12th century A.D.

Records a gift of 2 puṭṭis of land at Kannaripūṁḍi near Bhīmavrōlu for maintaining 12 lamps
in the temple of Bhīmēśvara by Maṁgūri Annaya.

inscription.head:
No. 27.
(A.R. No. B. 34 of 1953-54.)
Ēlūru, Eluru Taluk, West Godavari District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On pillar No. 9 in the compound of the Taluk Office.
The record is dated Śaka 11[3]0, corresponding to A.D. 1208-1209.

Damaged. Seems to register a lamp-stand weighing 42 vīsas and a gift of land for maintaining
a lamp in the Bhīmēśvara temple.

inscription.head:
No. 28.
(A.R. No. B. 35 of 1953-54.)
Amarāvati, Sattenapalle Taluk, Guntur District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a white marble railing at the site of the stūpa.

Undated. Written in the Prākṛit language and early Brāhmī script, datable to 2nd-1st century
B.C.
Records gift of a coping-stone (ushṇīsa) by the attendants of a princess named Saṁmalī.

edition.p: 1. Rājakumāriyā Saṁmaliyā parivēsakānaṁ unhisaṁ.

inscription.head:
No. 29.
(A.R. No. B. 36 of 1953-54.)
Cheñchu Eṟṟaguḍi, Dhone Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab in the compound of the Rāmaliṅgēśvara temple.
The record is dated Śaka 1489, Prabhava, Jyēshṭha, ba. 10, corresponding to A.D. 1567, June 2.

Registers the grant of the village Penumaḍa for the amṛitapaḍi service to god Yeṟṟaguḍi
Rāmaya (Rāmaliṅgēśvara) by Ponamarāju, son of Mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Pōchirāju-Rāmarāju of Viśvāmitra-
gōtra and solar race. The gift-village is stated to be situated in Dōni-sīma granted to the donor as
amara fief.

edition.p: Figures of crescent-moon, liṅga and sun.

l.te: 1489 ·agunēṭi prabhava || vacara(vatsara) jē(jyē)ṣṭa(ṣṭha) ba 10 lu śrīma[drā]jā-
marāju komāruḍu pōnamarājugāru daṁḍaṁ sama¨rpiṁci dhāravōsi yi-

ci[cci]na bhūdāna patra kramameṭlannanu [|*] [sa]tā(dā)si(śi)va dēvarāyalu māku
·amaranā-
yaṁkarānaku pāliṁcina dōni sīmalōni penumaḍagrāmaṁ yarragu-
ḍi rāmayadēvaraku sama¨rpiṁci yistimi [|*] viśvāmitra gōtra sūrya

edition.p:
Note: At the beginning of each line, a small circle is incised as a decorative motif. This practice
recurs in No. 30 below.

inscription.head:
No. 30.
(A.R. No. B. 37 of 1953-54.)
Cheñchu-Eṟṟaguḍi, Dhone Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab in the compound of the Rāmaliṅgēśvara temple.

The record is dated Śaka 1489, Prabhava, Jyēshṭha, śu. 11, Sunday corresponding to 1567 A.D.,
May 18.

Records the gift of a plot of land for the Pañchāmṛita and Pulukāpu services to god
Rāmēśvara of Yeṟaguḍi by Mahānāyakāchārya Mali-nāyiniṅgāru of Kambhālapāḍu for the merit of
his father Chavuḍi-nēni. The plot of land appears to have been given to the donor as a gift by the
villagers of Gummakoṇḍa and Teḷikimaṇḍavāḍa in appreciation of the settlement of a dispute between
the two by Mali-nāyuḍu. Reference is also made to one Veligōṭi Tim[m*]aya.

edition.p: Figures of Sun, linga and moon.

l.te: koṁḍa teḷiki maṁḍavāḍa yī reṁḍu vū(·ū)ḷlavāru po-
nāru ·ā polaṁ kha 1 nu mā taṁḍri cavuḍi nēniki
ci • • • • • • • • • • ṁ gummakoṁḍa reḍḍi karaṇālu māku
ṁku yiccina polaṁ kha | • • • • • mātaṁḍri cavuḍi nā-

inscription.head:
No. 31.
(A.R. No. B. 38 of 1953-54.)
Cheñchu-Eṟṟaguḍi, Dhone Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab in the compound of the Rāmaliṅgēśvara temple.

The record is dated Śaka 1486, Raktākshi, Kārtika, śu. 15, corresponding to A.D. 1564, October
19.

Registers the grant of a village situated in Kōṭikōṇa (?) - sīma by Mahāmaṇḍalēśvara
Koṇḍrājayyadēva-mahārāja, son of Timmarāja and grandson of Koṇḍreḍḍi of Gobbūru to god
Rāmēśvara of Yeṟṟaguḍi for the merit of Mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Rāmarāja-Timmarājayyadēva-mahārāja
from whom the donor is said to have obtained the territory as uṁbaḷi. The donor is stated to have
belonged to the Kāśyapa-gōtra, Āpastaṁba-sūtra and Yajuś-śākhā.

edition.p: Figures of an animal, liṅga, moon and sun.

l.te: ṭi catussīmalōgala nidhi nikṣēpa jala pāṣāṇa ·akṣīṇi ·āgā-
ci dhāravō[si*] yiccina [śi]lādha¨rma śāsanaṁ sāne(sāni) mānelu(mānulu)
ṁ bhavētu(t) || 2 || svadattāṁ vā paradattāṁ vā yō harēti
yatē krimiḥ || 3 || maṁgaḷamahā śrī śrī śrī jēyu-

edition.p: Below this inscription five lines are engraved in late characters.

inscription.head:
No. 32.
(A.R. No. B. 39 of 1953-54.)
Cheñchu Eṟṟaguḍi, Dhone Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab in the compound of the Rāmaliṅgēśvara temple.

The record is dated Śaka 1487, Krōdhana, Bhādrapada, śu. 2, Monday. The date is irregular,
may correspond to A.D. 1565, August 27 (Thursday).

Records the gift of some plots of land for various services in the temple of god Rāmēśvara
of Yeṟṟaguḍi by Mahānāyaṅkāchārya Mali-nāyuḍu of Kaṁbhālapāḍu for the merit of his father
Chevuḍi-nāyuḍu (Cf-No. 30 (A.R. 37 of 1953-54).

edition.p: Figures of moon and sun; Figures of liṅga, lamp-stand and bull facing the liṅga.

l.te: varimaḍi kha | vubhayaṁ kha ||| nnu reḍi praja-
vāriki yistimi saṁgāla grāmāna reḍi praja-

inscription.head:
No. 33.
(A.R. No. B. 41 of 1953-54.)
Iñjēḍu, Koilkuntla Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab in the Kalamdoḍḍi of Karrapu Sesha Reddi.
The record is dated Śaka 1461, Vikāri, Jyēshṭha, ba. 30, corresponding to 1539 A.D., June 16.

Records the construction of a step-well and the grant of some land for worship and offerings
to god Veṅkaṭādri of Iñjēḍu by Pāṇayya, son of Kēsavarāju of Iñjēḍu, and the sthala-karaṇam of
Iñjēḍu.

edition.p: Figures of crescent-moon and sun.
edition.l: daya śālivāhana śaka (figure of Conch is carved here).

l.te: na(tinna)vāru || maṁggaḷaṁ mahā śrī śrīṁ jē-

inscription.head:
No. 34.
(A.R. No. B. 42 of 1953-54.)
Paḍigipāḍu, Koilkuntla Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone in front of the Śiva temple.
No date. Written in the Telugu-Kannada characters of 8th-9th century A.D.

Badly damaged. Only a part of the praśasti commencing with Ari-durdhara-vara etc., is
preserved. As it refers to solar family, it might belong to the Rēnāṭi-Choḷa period.

edition.p: (i) Early lines in the 8th-9th century Telugu-Kannaḍa script.
(ii) Lines 13 and 14 contain letters of 11th-12th century A.D.

inscription.head:
No. 35.
(A.R. No. B. 44 of 1953-54.)
Uyyālavāḍa, Koilkuntla Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a broken slab in the village.
Undated. Written in Telugu characters of 15th-16th century A.D. (?).

The inscription is fragmentary and seems to record the campaigns of some king (name lost)
and refers to the consecration of a Śiva temple, and to the setting up of a pillar of victory. Mentions
Narasiṁha • • • • • [pā?]truḍu, the ūḷigam of the Mahārāja and to the gifts made by the former for
the Vasantotsava of Ahōbalēśvara. It could be an inscription of the Gajapatis of Cuttack, because they
were assisted by officers/generals whose names have the suffix Pātruḍu.

inscription.head:
No. 36.
(A.R. No. B. 45 of 1953-54.)
Narnūru, Kurnool Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab in front of the Mādhavasvāmin temple.
The record is dated in Kīlaka, Āśvija, śu. 15; Written in late characters.

Records the receipt of the principal with interest amounting to (9(1/2) rēkha-gadyas), issued
by Nadala-yallappa to Mālakoṇḍa Timmarāju and states that the loan deed was deemed cancelled
wherever it was.

inscription.head:
No. 37.
(A.R. No. B. 46 of 1953-54.)
Narnūru, Kurnool Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab in front of the maṇḍapa of the Mādhavasvāmin temple.
The record is dated Kīlaka, Āśvija, śu. 15; Written in late characters.

Records the receipt of the principal with interest issued by Nedhaṁ (Nadala?)-Yallappa to
Basuvaya and other Kāpus (Cf. No. 36 (A.R. 45 of 1953-54). Refers to the Chenna-kēśava temple.

inscription.head:
No. 38.
(A.R. No. B. 47 of 1953-54.)
Narnūru, Kurnool Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab in front of the Vīrabhadrasvāmin temple.

The record is dated Śaka 1467, Viśvāvasu, Āśvija, ba. 3, corresponding to 1545 A.D., September
23.

Records the settlement of a dispute among the priests of the Śaiva temples at Nariyanūru
and registers a grant to them by Narasiṅgayyadēva-mahārāja of Vōruvikaṁṭi, excempting them from
certain taxes to enable them to cultivate their lands. It refers to the desertion of the village by some
of the priests owing to some disputes; their grievance was set right by the intervention of the Chief.

l.te: tiṁmana vā • palli virā • • nā ma nāśiru
mī lō vicāri[raṁ] ceṁdaka maṁmmu saṁgamadē-

inscription.head:
No. 39.
(A.R. No. B. 48 of 1953-54.)
Narnūru, Kurnool Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone in the Nāgēśvara temple.
Undated. Palaeographically assignable to 10th century A.D.

Registers a grant of 20 maṟuturu of land to god Takshakēśvarabhaṭāra of Nariyanūru made
by the Three Hundred (munnūṟṟuvuru). Refers to a person named Nāga-Chaṇḍasvāmi-Tippaṇa-Pōtiya of
Sāṁkhya[?]-gōtra as the builder of the temple (?) and to the place as Kapila-maṭham. It is not clear
whether Pōtiya was the priest of the temple of Takshakēśvara. The gōtra might be Sāṅkhyāyana.

edition.p: Below is carved the figure of a liṅga.

inscription.head:
No. 40.
(A.R. No. B. 51 of 1953-54.)
Uḷchāla, Kurnool Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab near the Kāḷamma temple.
Dated in Pramādi, Kārttika, śu. 1.

Records the rates of lease of agricultural lands leased out to the Reḍḍi-prajas of Vuluchala
by Mahānāyaṅkāchārya Rājavōli Mallinēniṅgāru at one gadya and two chakras for every unit of
land of one tūmu bījavari.

l.te: vulu ca¨rllanu cēsē guttapolaṁ mīku rēgaḍu
cēnu bījavari é1 tūmeḍu bījavari polānaku
vara ga 1 cakrar 21 ·ubhayaṁ varahā ga 1 cakra

inscription.head:
No. 41.
(A.R. No. B. 52 of 1953-54.)
Uḷchāla, Kurnool Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab near the centre of the village.
Dated in Śaka 1477, Ānanda, Phālguṇa, śu. 15, corresponding to 1555 A.D., March 8.

Registers a grant of the Vipravinōdi-Varttana collected by the Vipravinōdins from the
Brāhmaṇas by Mantramūrti Pina-Basuvaya, (grandson of) Gangādhara-Voḍeyala, (son of)
Pina-Vīramarāju of Āpastaṁba-sūtra and Yajus-śākhā of Bhāradvāja-gōtra, to god Hanumanta of
Vuluchala in the Kandanavōli-sīma for the daily offering of lights (dīpārādhana) to the god. It is
also stated that the gift was given for the merit of Vipravinōdins viz., Dakshiṇāmūrti, Kēśavayya,
Vallabhayya, Varadayya, Chāgalamaṟṟi Parvatayya and Anantayya.

edition.p: Figures of crescent-moon, ḍamaru, liṅga, nandi and sun.

l.te: da saṁvatsara phālguṇa śu 15 lu śrīmadrājādhi-
rāja rājaparamēśvara śrī vīrapratāpa śrīvīra sadāśivadēvama-
sāmrājyaṁ śē(cē)stuṁḍagānu kaṁ[da]navōli sīmalōni vulucala hanu-

l.note: From here, the writing on the right side of the stone has peeled off.

inscription.head:
No. 42.
(A.R. No. B. 53 of 1953-54.)
Uḷchāla, Kurnool Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab near the centre of the village.
Dated in Śaka 1480, Kāḷayukti, Māgha, śu. 7, corresponding to 1559 A.D., January 16.

Registers a bhaṭa-mānya of land sowable with one tūmu of seed to Bhaṭṭa-Tippayya
by Mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Māḍrāju Narasarājayyadēva-māhārāja. The gift land lay in Vuluchala
situated in Kandanōli (Kandanavōli)-sīma granted to the donor as amara-nāyaṅkara by Rāmarāju
Veṅkaṭādrirājayyadēva-mahārāja.

edition.p: Figures of crecent-moon and sun.

l.note: To the right of this line and that of lines 8-10, the stone has peeled

li teruvuku vu(·u)ttaraṁ jaṁmmimāni[sthaḷa] bījavari • 1 tū[meṁḍu]
vittanālu pārēṭi polaṁ nīku • • • • • timinī putra [pautra]

l.note: Right portion in lines 14-18 has faded out.

inscription.head:
No. 43.
(A.R. No. B. 55 of 1953-54.)
Santa-jūṭūru, Nandyal Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone near the temple of Rāmaliṅgēśvara on the outskirts of the village.
Dated in Śaka 1306, Raktākshi, Māgha, śu. 5, Monday, corresponding to 1385 A.D., January 16.

Records the construction of a well for providing water for the sacred bath of the god and
the attendant deities by the palanquin-bearer (besta) Nāgana-bōya, son of Yelle-bōya.

edition.p: Figures of liṅga, nandi and crescent-moon.

inscription.head:
No. 44.
(A.R. No. B. 63 of 1953-54.)
Rājula-Maṇḍagiri (hamlet of Jūṭūru), Pattikonda Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a rock near the pond close to the Rāmaliṅgēśvara temple.
Undated. Written in the Prākṛit language and Brāhmī script of 3rd century B.C.

It contains two Minor Rock Edicts of the king Aśoka, varying slightly from the version in
the Eṟṟaguḍi edict.
(Inscription published in Epigraphia Indica, Vol. XXXI, pp. 211 ff).

inscription.head:
No. 45.
(A.R. No. B. 66 of 1953-54.)
Rājula-Maṇḍagiri, Pattikonda Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a rock near the pond close to the Rāmaliṅgēśvara temple.
Dated in Śaka 155[5], Māgha, ba. 5, corresponding to 1634 A.D. February 8.

Records the grant of some mānya land for maintaining a perpetual lamp in the temple of
Kēśava at Maṇḍagiri by Chennaya, grandson of Vināyaka[llaya].

edition.p: Figures of conch, crescent-moon, sun and discus.

inscription.head:
No. 46.
(A.R. No. B. 67 of 1953-54.)
Rājula-Maṇḍagiri, Pattikonda Taluk, Kurnool District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a rock near the pond close to the Rāmaliṅgēśvara temple.
Dated in Śaka [1738], Akshaya, [Bhādrapada, ba 7]; written in late characters.
Completely damaged and worn out.

edition.p: There are some letters on the right side of all the lines, but not distinct.

inscription.head:
No. 47.
(A.R. No. B. 69 of 1953-54.)
Chējarla, Atmakur Taluk, Nellore District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab in front of the Chennakēśava temple.

Dated in Śaka 16[1]9, Īśvara, Jyēshṭha, ba. 30, Bhṛiguvāra, solar eclipse. There was no solar
eclipse in Jyēshṭha, nor was ba. 30, a Friday. Two solar eclipses occurred in this year in Chaitra and
Āśvīja months. The corresponding date could be 1697 A.D., June 8 (no eclipse; Tuesday).

Records grants to the temple of Chennakēśava of Cheṁjerla and to Nāgāvojhula-Timmāvojhulu
by Vēmā reḍḍi of Sarvapalli who bore the title Hindurāyasuratrāṇa. (Published in Inscriptions of
Nellore District, Part-I, Atmakuru, No. 23).

edition.p: Figures of sun, conch, tīrunāmam, discus and crescent-moon.

l.te: ·agunēṁṭi yī(·ī)śvara saṁvatsara jēṣṭa(ṣṭha) ba 30 bhṛguvāra sū[rya]-

inscription.head:
No. 48.
(A.R. No. B. 70 of 1953-54.)
Muruguḷḷa, Atmakur Taluk, Nellore District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a stone in a haystack near the Śiva temple.
Dated in Śaka [11]76 (not verifiable). (A.D. 1254-55). In characters of 13th century A.D.

Damaged. States that, when Tirukāḷattidēva having ruled the mortal world, went away to rule
over the celestial world (died), his subordinate (name lost) made a gift of the village of Muṟuguḍlu
to god Viśvanāthadēva for the merit of his lord.

edition.p: Figures of crescent-moon and sun.

inscription.head:
No. 49.
(A.R. No. B. 71 of 1953-54.)
Muruguḷḷa, Atmakur Taluk, Nellore District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a Nandi-stone in front of the ruined temple of Śiva, north-west of the village.
Undated. Written in late characters; and the size of the letters is large.
States that the land is the mānya of Mallēśvarasvāmin.

inscription.head:
No. 50.
(A.R. No. B. 74 of 1953-54.)
Kandukūru, Kandukuru Taluk, Nellore District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On a slab lying in the compound of the Taluk office.
Dated in Śaka 1244 • • • • ., Chaitra, śu. 5, Monday, corresponding to 1322 A.D., March 22.

Fragmentary. Seems to record a gift of land to god Kalikēśvaradēva of Kandukūru. Mentions
Kāmaya Boppaniṁgāru, and Rudradēva, the latter probably as the donor.

edition.p: Figures of sun, crescent-moon and liṅga.

l.te: saṁvatsara caitra śu 5 sō [|*] svasti śrīma[nma] • • • • •

inscription.head:
No. 51.
(A.R. No. B. 77 of 1953-54.)
Nellore, Nellore Taluk, Nellore District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On the lintel at the entrance to the Irukulamma temple.
Dated in Pramādi, Mārgaśira, śu. 10. (not verifiable); written in late characters.
States that the gōpura was the work of Timmayya, son of Timmarasu of Chōḷasamudram.

l.te: ṁ śubhamastu | pramādi saṁvvatsara mā¨rggaśira śuddha 10 lu |

inscription.head:
No. 52.
(A.R. No. B. 80 of 1953-54.)
Nellore, Nellore Taluk, Nellore District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On the wall of the Irukulamma temple.

Dated in Śaka 1286, Krōdhi, Mārgaśira, ba. 5, Friday, corresponding to 1364 A.D., December
13.

Damaged. Records the decision of the nānādēśīs who seem to have granted certain
levies collected at the Friday-market established by Kāchaṇṇa of Nellore, to the goddess [Eraku]lā-
paramēśvarī.
(Published in Inscriptions of the Nellore District, Part II, No. 78, p. 848).

inscription.head:
No. 53.
(A.R. No. B. 81 of 1953-54.)
Nellore, Nellore Taluk, Nellore District, Andhra Pradesh.
inscription.p: On the beam of the temple of Irukalamma.

Dated in Śaka 1237, Ānanda, Bhādrapada, śu. • • corresponding to 1315 A.D., August 1st to 15th
(Śaka 1237 corresponds to Rākshasa).

Damaged. Seems to record a gift (details lost) by Nāgaya, son of Haridēva who was a gaṇa
-bhṛitya and some boyas to the goddess Irukalā-Paramēśvarī. Invokes prosperity to Muppiḍi-nāyaka
and Peda-Rudradēva.
